Systems: guardrail (top rail 42 inches plus or minus 3 inches, midrail at 21 inches, withstand 200-lb force), personal fall arrest system (PFAS), or safety net. PFAS components: full-body harness, lanyard or self-retracting lifeline (SRL), anchorage rated at 5,000 lbs per worker. PFAS must arrest fall within 3.5 feet, max arresting force 1,800 lbs. Shock-absorbing lanyard extends to 6 ft plus deceleration distance. SRL (self-retracting lifeline): auto-locks, much shorter fall distance than lanyard. Leading edge work: SRL or specific leading-edge rated PFAS required. Scaffolding: 10 ft trigger height (Subpart L). Excavation spoil edge: 2-ft setback is a tripping hazard.

chunks.title: OSHA Lockout/Tagout -- Energy Control for Construction
chunks.content: OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147 and 29 CFR 1926.417 govern control of hazardous energy during maintenance and servicing. Sequence: notify affected employees, identify and locate all energy sources, shut down equipment, isolate all energy (shut valves, disconnect electrical, block mechanical), apply personal lock and danger tag to each energy source, release or restrain stored energy (capacitors, springs, compressed air, gravity), verify by attempting to start. Each worker applies their own lock -- never rely on another person's lock for protection. Multi-hasp allows multiple locks on single isolation point. Tagout only permitted if lock application is not possible. Re-energization: remove tools and materials, employees clear, remove locks in reverse order. Applies to pump stations, traffic control systems, electrical panels.

chunks.title: OSHA Struck-By Hazards -- Vehicle and Equipment Safety
chunks.content: Struck-by incidents are the second leading cause of construction fatalities. Four types: struck by flying object, struck by falling object, struck by swinging equipment, struck by vehicle. Prevention: establish clear equipment exclusion zones, never allow workers in the swing radius of excavators or cranes. Operator must have clear sight lines or use a spotter. Spotter required when equipment backs up or changes direction near workers. High-visibility vest Class 2 minimum near equipment, Class 3 near moving traffic. Barricade work zone from vehicle traffic per MUTCD standards. Backup alarms required on all equipment. Spotters must be a designated, trained person. Suspended loads: stay clear of load path at all times. Tag lines to control load swing.

chunks.title: OSHA Heat Illness Prevention -- Field Protocol for Hot Weather Work
chunks.content: Heat illness is preventable. OSHA recommends water-rest-shade protocol. Water: 1 cup (8 oz) every 15-20 minutes during heat exposure, cold water preferred. Shade: rest area must be nearby, shaded or air-conditioned. Rest: schedule breaks during hottest part of day (10 AM - 2 PM), rotate crews. Acclimatization: new workers must be gradually exposed to heat over 14 days -- most heat fatalities occur in first few days. Warning signs of heat exhaustion: heavy sweating, weakness, cold and pale skin, fast weak pulse, nausea. Heat stroke (emergency): high body temperature (103+ F), hot red dry skin, rapid strong pulse, confusion -- call 911, cool immediately. WBGT (Wet Bulb Globe Temperature) index accounts for humidity, sun, and air temperature. OSHA General Duty Clause applies in all states.

chunks.title: Concrete Material Pricing -- Ready-Mix, CDF, and Flowable Fill (2024)
chunks.content: Approximate concrete material prices, western US 2024. Ready-mix 3000 psi: $150-$185/CY. 4000 psi: $165-$200/CY. 5000 psi: $180-$220/CY. Fiber-reinforced add $8-$12/CY. Short load charge (less than 8 CY): $15-$25. Overtime and Saturday delivery add 10-20%. Controlled Density Fill (CDF or flowable fill): $90-$130/CY -- self-leveling, excavatable, used for utility trench abandonment and structure backfill. Lean concrete base: $110-$145/CY. Shotcrete (wet mix): $250-$400/CY installed. Grout (non-shrink): $30-$60 per 50-lb bag. Concrete pump mobilization: $800-$1,500/day plus per-yard charge. Winter concrete: cold weather protection add $15-$30/CY. These are material-only prices -- add forming, labor, and equipment for all-in cost.

chunks.title: Asphalt Material Pricing -- HMA Tonnage and Surface Rates (2024)
chunks.content: Hot mix asphalt (HMA) pricing varies significantly by oil price index and region. Western US 2024 approximate: HMA 3/4-inch surface mix: $90-$135/ton. HMA 1-inch base course: $80-$125/ton. Open-graded friction course: $100-$145/ton. Warm mix asphalt (WMA): add $5-$10/ton. SMA (stone matrix asphalt): add $15-$25/ton. Liquid asphalt (AC-20 or PG 64-22): $700-$950/ton -- oil index drives everything. Tack coat: $0.10-$0.20/SY. Crack seal: $0.50-$1.50/LF. Trench patch per SY: 2-inch overlay $15-$25/SY material only. Asphalt millings (recycled): $8-$18/ton, usable for base or temporary access. Compacted density: 110-115 lbs/SY per inch of thickness. Rule of thumb: 1 ton covers approximately 50 SY at 2 inches compacted.

chunks.title: Pipe Material Pricing -- PVC, HDPE, Ductile Iron by Size (2024)
chunks.content: Approximate pipe material prices, western US 2024 (20-ft sticks, FOB distributor, not installed). PVC C900 water main: 6-inch DR18 $12-$18/LF, 8-inch $18-$27/LF, 12-inch $32-$48/LF. PVC SDR 35 sewer: 6-inch $6-$10/LF, 8-inch $10-$15/LF, 12-inch $18-$28/LF. HDPE DR11: 6-inch $14-$20/LF, 8-inch $22-$32/LF, 12-inch $40-$58/LF. Ductile iron class 52: 6-inch $28-$40/LF, 8-inch $40-$58/LF, 12-inch $70-$95/LF, 16-inch $110-$150/LF. RCP class III: 18-inch $35-$55/LF, 24-inch $55-$80/LF, 36-inch $90-$130/LF. Corrugated HDPE storm: 12-inch $6-$10/LF, 18-inch $12-$18/LF, 24-inch $20-$30/LF. Prices increase with oil index, tariffs, and freight. Fittings and specials typically add 15-25% to straight run cost.

chunks.title: Aggregate Material Pricing -- Base Course, Select Fill, Bedding (2024)
chunks.content: Aggregate pricing varies by quarry distance and haul. Western US 2024 approximate, FOB quarry or pit. 3/4-inch crushed gravel (road base, Class 6): $14-$22/ton. 1.5-inch base course: $12-$20/ton. 3/8-inch pea gravel (pipe bedding): $18-$28/ton. Concrete sand (bedding): $16-$25/ton. Select fill (pit run gravel): $8-$16/ton. Native select (screened, no organics): $5-$12/ton. Rip rap D50=12-inch: $35-$55/ton. Rip rap D50=24-inch: $40-$60/ton. Recycled concrete base: $8-$14/ton. Haul cost: $4-$8/ton/mile, 15-20 miles typical economical radius from quarry. Bank measure to compacted measure: 1.15-1.30 shrinkage factor for granular fill. Swell factor for excavated material: 1.10-1.35 depending on soil type.

chunks.title: Water Main Fittings and Valves -- Pricing Reference (2024)
chunks.content: Approximate material pricing for water main fittings and appurtenances, western US 2024. Ductile iron fittings (MJ): 6-inch 90-degree elbow $85-$130, 8-inch $140-$210, 12-inch $280-$420. Gate valves (resilient wedge, AWWA C509): 6-inch $350-$550, 8-inch $600-$900, 12-inch $1,400-$2,000. Butterfly valves (AWWA C504, 10-inch and larger): 10-inch $600-$900, 12-inch $800-$1,200, 16-inch $1,400-$2,200. Fire hydrant (3-way, 5-1/4-inch main valve): $1,500-$2,800 depending on brand and bury depth. Megalug retainer glands: 6-inch $45-$75 set, 8-inch $65-$110, 12-inch $120-$180. Air release valve (1-inch): $200-$450. Blow-off assembly (2-inch): $400-$700. Corporation stops (3/4-inch): $25-$45 each.

chunks.title: Steel and Rebar Material Pricing -- Structural and Reinforcing (2024)
chunks.content: Structural and reinforcing steel pricing, US 2024. Reinforcing bar (rebar): #4 (1/2-inch) $0.65-$0.90/lb, #5 (5/8-inch) $0.65-$0.90/lb, #6 (3/4-inch) $0.65-$0.90/lb. Rebar is sold by weight: #4 = 0.668 lb/LF, #5 = 1.043 lb/LF, #6 = 1.502 lb/LF. Welded wire reinforcement (WWR): $0.20-$0.45/SF. Structural steel (wide flange beams): $0.90-$1.30/lb fabricated. Plate steel: $0.80-$1.20/lb. HSS tube: $0.90-$1.20/lb. Sheet pile (steel): $35-$60/SF of wall face depending on section. Trench plate (road plate): rental $5-$15/day per plate or buy at $600-$1,200 each (4x8 to 4x12). Steel prices are volatile -- tied to iron ore and scrap markets. Fabricated structural steel order lead times: 8-16 weeks typical.

chunks.title: Water and Sewer Main Installation -- Step-by-Step Field Workflow
chunks.content: Standard underground utility pipe installation sequence: 1. Locate existing utilities (811, vacuum excavate to verify at conflicts). 2. Establish grade stakes from survey shots, mark every 50-100 ft. 3. Open cut trench to design depth plus bedding depth. 4. Shape trench bottom, remove soft spots, compact if disturbed. 5. Place bedding material (typically 4-inch minimum), strike to grade with laser or grade rod. 6. Set pipe: clean bell and spigot, lubricate gasket, pull joint home, check deflection angle. 7. Verify invert elevation with laser or grade rod at each joint. 8. Place haunching material, hand tamp both sides simultaneously to avoid shifting pipe. 9. Initial backfill to 12-inch above crown, compact in lifts. 10. Continue to next pipe section. 11. Final backfill in compacted lifts per spec. 12. Pressure test (water main) or mandrel and camera (sewer) before final backfill to subgrade.

chunks.title: Water Main Pressure Testing -- AWWA Hydrostatic Test Procedure
chunks.content: AWWA C600 pressure test requirements for water mains. Test pressure: 1.5 times working pressure, minimum 150 psi, held for 2 hours. Acceptable leakage formula: L = ND(P^0.5) / 7,400 where L = allowable leakage GPH, N = number of joints, D = nominal pipe diameter (inches), P = average test pressure (psi). Procedure: fill main slowly, bleed air at all high points through air release valves or open hydrants. Pressurize to test pressure with hand pump or pressure test pump. Monitor for 2 hours. Record pressure at start and end. Repair all visible leaks regardless of allowable leakage formula. Disinfection (AWWA C651): chlorinate at 25 ppm minimum, 24-hour contact time, then flush and test. Two consecutive passing bacteriological samples before placing in service.

chunks.title: Sewer Testing -- Mandrel, Air Test, and CCTV Inspection
chunks.content: Gravity sewer testing requirements. Low-pressure air test (ASTM C828): pressurize to 4.0 psi, let stabilize 2 minutes, then monitor for pressure drop. Pass criteria: pressure must not drop more than 1.0 psi in the time specified by diameter and length. Infiltration test: measure flow into pipeline -- must not exceed specification (typically 200 gallons per inch-diameter per mile per day). Deflection (mandrel) test: 9-sided rigid mandrel pulled through pipe to verify roundness. Mandrel diameter typically 95% of nominal inside diameter. Must be performed 30 days after backfill to allow soil to consolidate. CCTV inspection: camera run through completed main, record condition and verify grade. Identifies deflection, offset joints, sags, root intrusion. All testing must pass before city acceptance.

chunks.title: Daily Production Tracking -- LF Installed vs Budget
chunks.content: Track pipe installation daily: record start station, end station, and pipe size installed by end of shift. Calculate linear feet installed for the day. Compare to bid production rate. If bid assumed 300 LF/day and crew did 180, identify cause immediately (unexpected rock, extra fittings, dewatering delay, crew shortage). Daily foreman report should capture: LF installed by size, manholes set, service laterals, fittings installed, equipment hours, crew headcount, delays and causes. Weekly totals against schedule: are you on pace to hit milestone dates? Earned value simple metric: (LF installed / total bid LF) x total contract value = earned contract value. Compare to amount spent. A good crew should track within 10-15% of bid production in normal conditions. Deviations beyond that warrant a change order or schedule recovery plan.

chunks.title: Change Order Documentation -- What to Capture and How to Price
chunks.content: A change order documents and prices work outside the original contract scope. Key documentation triggers: owner-directed change, differing site conditions (DSC), unforeseen underground conflicts, design changes by engineer. Document in real time: daily log entries noting extra work, equipment deployed, crew hours, materials used. Photograph everything -- existing conditions before you disturb them, the obstruction, the conflict, the repair. Send written notice per contract requirements (often 24-48 hours for DSC, 7-14 days for other changes). T&M change order pricing: direct labor at payroll cost plus burden (typically 35-50% of base wage), equipment at ownership or rental rate, material at invoice cost. Markup: overhead 10-15%, profit 5-15%, bond and insurance 1-3%. Never do extra work without written authorization unless safety or emergency requires it.

chunks.title: 811 Bluestakes Process -- Step-by-Step for Utah and Western States
chunks.content: Federal law requires calling 811 before any digging. Process: 1. Call 811 or submit online at utiliquest.com or call811.com at least 3 business days before digging. 2. Provide: excavation address, dig site description, start date, contact info, type of work, and depth if known. 3. Utilities receive notification and mark within 2 business days. 4. Locate marks appear on ground in APWA color codes. 5. Do not dig within 18-24 inches of any mark without potholing (vacuum excavate) to verify exact location and depth. 6. Locate marks are valid for 14 business days in Utah. Renew before expiration on long jobs. 7. If no marks appear by start date, call back for status before digging. 8. Positive response required: utilities must respond with marked, no conflict, or unable to locate. Emergency excavations: call 811 and can begin after 2 hours in life or safety emergency.

chunks.title: RFI and Submittal Process -- Construction Documentation Workflow
chunks.content: RFI (Request for Information) is a formal written question from contractor to engineer or owner asking for clarification on design, specifications, or conflicts. RFI best practices: number sequentially, describe the issue clearly, reference plan sheet and spec section, propose a solution if possible, state the cost and schedule impact. Owner or engineer typically has 14-21 days to respond per contract. Track all open RFIs by due date -- unanswered RFIs are a schedule risk. Submittals: contractor submits product data, shop drawings, or material samples for engineer approval before fabrication or delivery. Typical submittal lead time: 3-6 weeks. Submit early -- owner has 14-21 days to review. Re-submittals reset the clock. Procore, Autodesk ACC, PlanGrid, and Bluebeam Studio are common platforms. Document every RFI and submittal response -- they become part of the contract record and support future claims.

chunks.title: Proctor Compaction Test -- Standard, Modified, and Field Testing
chunks.content: Proctor compaction test determines maximum dry density (MDD) and optimum moisture content (OMC) for a given soil. Standard Proctor (ASTM D698): 3 lifts, 25 blows per lift with 5.5-lb hammer, 12-inch drop. Modified Proctor (ASTM D1557): 5 lifts, 25 blows with 10-lb hammer -- higher energy, higher MDD, lower OMC. Modified Proctor is required for most roadway and utility trench work. Relative compaction (RC) = (field dry density / MDD) x 100. Typical specs: 95% Modified Proctor under roadways, 90% in non-traffic areas, 92% in utility trench backfill zones. Field testing: nuclear density gauge (Troxler, Humboldt), sand cone (ASTM D1556), drive cylinder (ASTM D2937). Failing test: reduce lift thickness, adjust moisture, increase passes. Clay must be within plus or minus 2% of OMC to achieve target compaction.

chunks.title: Soil Volume Conversion -- Bank, Loose, and Compacted Measure
chunks.content: Soil volume changes state when disturbed. Three states: Bank (in-place, undisturbed), Loose (excavated, loaded in truck), Compacted (placed and compacted fill). Conversion factors: Bank to Loose multiply by swell factor. Loose to Compacted multiply by shrinkage factor. Common swell factors: clay 25-35%, sandy clay 20-30%, sand 10-15%, gravel 5-10%, rock 35-50% (blasted). Common shrinkage factors (compacted vs bank): clay fill 5-15% shrinkage, granular fill 8-12% shrinkage, rock fill 25-35% shrinkage. Example: 1,000 CY bank measure clay becomes 1,300 CY loose (in trucks) and becomes 850 CY compacted in fill. Bid trench excavation in bank CY. Import fill in compacted CY. A 14 CY truck body holds 14 loose CY, which is only about 10-11 bank CY of clay.

chunks.title: Unit Price Bid Structure -- How to Build a Bid Item
chunks.content: Unit price contracts price each bid item per unit of work (LF, CY, EA, ton). Each unit price must cover all costs to complete that item. Build-up method: start with direct labor (crew hours x loaded wage rate), add equipment (ownership or rental rate x production time), add material (quantity x unit cost plus waste), add subcontractor cost, then apply overhead and profit markup. Overhead: job-specific (superintendent, PM, safety, small tools, fuel, mobilization) plus company overhead allocation. Overhead typically 15-25% of direct cost. Profit: 5-15% depending on competition, risk, and backlog. Bond: 1-2.5% of bid price. Example for 8-inch PVC water main: material $20/LF plus labor $18/LF plus equipment $15/LF = $53 direct cost, add 20% overhead ($10.60) plus 10% profit ($6.36) plus 1.5% bond ($1.05) = $71/LF bid price.

chunks.title: Crew Cost Calculation -- Loaded Labor Rate and Daily Cost
chunks.content: Loaded labor rate includes base wage plus all employer-paid costs. Burden components: FICA payroll tax (7.65%), FUTA and SUTA unemployment (varies, approximately 3%), workers compensation insurance (heavy civil: 12-25% of base wage depending on classification), general liability insurance (1-3%), health benefits, retirement match, vacation and holiday accrual, training. Total burden: 35-55% of base wage for underground utility work. Example: $35/hr base wage x 1.50 burden factor = $52.50/hr loaded rate. Crew composition for 8-inch water main: 1 foreman ($40/hr base, $62 loaded), 2 pipefitters ($35/hr, $52 loaded), 2 laborers ($28/hr, $44 loaded), 1 operator ($48/hr, $72 loaded). Total crew: $62 plus $104 plus $88 plus $72 = $326/hr crew cost. At 300 LF/day and 10-hr shift: $3,260/day crew labor, $10.87/LF labor cost.

chunks.title: Quantity Takeoff -- Trench Excavation, Pipe, and Backfill
chunks.content: Quantity takeoff for underground utility work. Pipe quantity: measure centerline length from plan, add LF for fittings (tees add pipe equivalent length). Add 3-5% for waste and couplings. Trench excavation: volume = trench width x average depth x length. Trench width: pipe OD plus 18 inches minimum each side for working room. Average depth: (start depth plus end depth) / 2 per run. Convert cubic feet to cubic yards: divide by 27. Add 10-15% for over-excavation and cleanup. Bedding quantity: bedding volume = width x bedding depth x length / 27. Import select backfill: total trench volume minus bedding, minus pipe volume, minus native re-use. Native re-use limited by spec -- usually only 3 ft below surface layer. Asphalt surface restoration: length x road width x thickness, convert using density factor (ton).

chunks.title: Pipe Flow Capacity -- GPM by Diameter at Common Velocities
chunks.content: Water main flow capacity at common design velocities. Flow (GPM) = velocity (ft/s) x pipe area (SF) x 448.8. At 5 ft/s (normal design): 4-inch = 196 GPM, 6-inch = 441 GPM, 8-inch = 784 GPM, 10-inch = 1,225 GPM, 12-inch = 1,763 GPM, 16-inch = 3,134 GPM. At 8 ft/s (fire flow design): 8-inch = 1,254 GPM, 12-inch = 2,821 GPM, 16-inch = 5,015 GPM. Maximum velocity for water main: 10 ft/s to avoid water hammer. Minimum velocity for sewer (self-cleaning): 2.0 ft/s at design flow. Manning's equation for gravity flow: Q = (1.486/n) x A x R^(2/3) x S^(1/2). For PVC (n=0.010): 8-inch at 0.40% slope = 0.62 CFS = 278 GPM at half-full flow.

chunks.title: Head Loss and Friction -- Hazen-Williams and Darcy-Weisbach
chunks.content: Pressure loss in water mains calculated by Hazen-Williams: hL = 10.67 x L x Q^1.852 / (C^1.852 x D^4.87), where hL = head loss (ft), L = pipe length (ft), Q = flow (CFS), C = Hazen-Williams coefficient, D = diameter (ft). C values: PVC new 150, ductile iron cement-lined 130, cast iron old 80-100. Darcy-Weisbach is more rigorous: hL = f x (L/D) x (V^2/2g), where f = friction factor from Moody diagram. Minor losses at fittings: gate valve (open) 0.1V^2/2g, 90-degree elbow 0.9V^2/2g, tee (branch) 1.8V^2/2g. Pressure drop rule of thumb: 2-5 psi per 1,000 ft in a well-designed distribution system at normal flow. Water hammer pressure spike: approximately 50-60 psi per ft/s of velocity for rapid valve closure.

chunks.title: Stormwater System Design -- Rational Method and Pipe Sizing
chunks.content: Rational method for stormwater peak flow: Q = C x i x A. Q = peak flow (CFS), C = runoff coefficient (0.2 for lawn, 0.7 for paved surface, 0.9 for impervious), i = rainfall intensity (in/hr) from IDF curve at design storm return period (2, 10, 25, or 100-year), A = drainage area (acres). IDF curves are region-specific -- get from local jurisdiction or NOAA Atlas 14. For urban areas, 10-year storm is typical for storm drain sizing. 100-year storm for major culverts and detention sizing. Manning's equation for storm pipe sizing: at full-pipe flow, match Q from Rational Method. Storm drain velocity: 2.0 ft/s minimum (self-cleaning), 15 ft/s maximum (prevent scour). Retention vs detention: retention holds runoff permanently (pond infiltrates), detention holds temporarily and releases at controlled rate.

chunks.title: Construction Contract Types -- Lump Sum, Unit Price, T&M, and GMP
chunks.content: Lump sum (fixed price): contractor agrees to complete defined scope for a set price. Contractor bears quantity risk. Unit price: contractor prices each bid item per unit (LF, CY, ton, EA). Owner bears quantity risk -- used when quantities are uncertain (underground utilities, earthwork). Time and materials (T&M): contractor paid for actual labor, equipment, and material plus agreed markup. Owner bears all risk. Used for emergency work, unforeseen conditions, or scope that cannot be defined. GMP (Guaranteed Maximum Price): cost-plus with an agreed ceiling. Contractor shares savings and bears overrun risk above GMP. Used in construction manager at-risk (CMAR) delivery. Design-Build (D-B): single entity responsible for both design and construction. Owner gets single point of responsibility. Progressive Design-Build (PDB): owner selects team early, design and construction proceed concurrently.

chunks.title: Subcontractor Bid Process -- Scope, Leveling, and Award
chunks.content: Process for obtaining and evaluating subcontractor bids. Scope sheet: provide written scope document specifying exactly what is and is not included. Send to at least 3 qualified subs per trade. Request subs submit 24-48 hours before GC bid due time to allow leveling. Bid leveling: create apples-to-apples comparison spreadsheet. Check each sub's scope inclusion -- one may exclude traffic control, another may exclude restoration. Call subs to clarify gaps before plugging into estimate. Sub qualification: verify license, insurance (request certificate naming GC as additional insured), bonding capacity, and past work. Award: provide written subcontract with clear scope, schedule milestones, payment terms, and retainage. Flow-down clauses: prime contract risk provisions flow down to subcontract.

chunks.title: Horizontal Directional Drilling -- HDD Planning and Execution
chunks.content: HDD bores a curved path underground to install pipe without open cutting. Phases: pilot bore (steerable drill head follows planned path), pre-ream (enlarge hole in passes to pipe OD plus 25-50%), pullback (pipe pulled through). Entry angle: typically 8-16 degrees, exit angle similar. Radius of curvature: minimum radius depends on pipe material -- HDPE DR11 minimum radius is 1,200 x pipe OD, steel 40-50 x OD. Pilot bore tracking: walkover (magnetic beacon, less accurate), wireline (through drill string, more accurate for deep bores). Drilling fluid (mud): bentonite slurry keeps bore open, cools bit, lifts cuttings. Frac-out: drilling fluid breaks to surface -- contamination risk, stop and treat. HDD soil conditions: sand and gravel good, cobbles difficult, boulders can deflect bit. Existing utilities: maintain 2-ft minimum from bore path to any existing utility.

chunks.title: Microtunneling and Pipe Jacking -- Trenchless Installation Method
chunks.content: Microtunneling is a remote-controlled, guided boring method for installing pipe without entering the bore. A microtunnel boring machine (MTBM) is launched from a shaft and excavates while pipe is jacked from behind. Sizes: 6-inch through 120-inch diameter. Accuracy: can achieve plus or minus 0.1% grade over long runs -- used for gravity sewer where slope is critical. Pipe jacking forces increase with distance and soil friction -- large projects require intermediate jacking stations (IJS). Pipe materials: reinforced concrete jacking pipe, steel jacking pipe, HDPE with casing. Works in all soils including rock, groundwater, running sand (uses face pressure to control). Launch and reception shafts: typically 12-20 ft diameter. More expensive than open cut but eliminates traffic disruption, deep excavation, and surface impact. Common in road crossings, railroad crossings, waterway crossings, urban utility corridors.

chunks.title: Construction Scheduling -- CPM, Critical Path, and Schedule Types
chunks.content: Critical Path Method (CPM) is the standard for construction scheduling. Activities are linked by dependencies; the critical path is the longest sequence of dependent activities from start to finish. Float (slack): non-critical activities have float -- days they can slip without delaying completion. Total float is shared among activities in the chain. Activity duration: estimated from production rates and crew size. Schedule types: baseline (original bid schedule), as-built (what actually happened), recovery schedule (revised plan after delay). Primavera P6 is the industry standard for large projects. Microsoft Project is common for smaller work. Short-interval schedule (3-week look-ahead): crew-level schedule updated weekly, drives daily work. Delay types: excusable compensable (owner-caused, contractor gets time and money), excusable non-compensable (weather, gets time but no money), inexcusable (contractor-caused, no relief).

chunks.title: Erosion and Sediment Control -- SWPPP and Common BMP Methods
chunks.content: Stormwater Pollution Prevention Plan (SWPPP) is required for construction sites disturbing 1 or more acres under EPA NPDES Construction General Permit. Utah: apply to Utah Division of Water Quality for CGP coverage before land disturbance. Common BMPs: Silt fence: fabric staked along contour, traps sediment from sheet flow -- not for concentrated flow. Inlet protection: filter bags, sediment filters for catch basins. Fiber rolls (wattles): rolled straw or compost, laid on contour or in swale, slows flow. Rock check dams: in swales and ditches, spread flow and trap sediment. Stabilized construction entrance: 6-inch rock pad to knock mud off tires before exiting site. Slope matting or blanket: erosite or coconut fiber mat over seeded slopes. Hydroseeding: seed mixed in slurry, sprayed on disturbed slopes, must be applied within 14 days of final grade in Utah. Concrete washout: lined pit, never discharge concrete wash to ground.

chunks.title: Traffic Control -- MUTCD Standards for Construction Zones
chunks.content: All temporary traffic control on public roads must comply with MUTCD (Manual on Uniform Traffic Control Devices). Key components: advance warning area, transition area, activity area (work zone), termination area. Taper length formula: L = WS^2/60 for speeds under 45 mph, L = WS for speeds 45 mph and above. L = taper length (ft), W = lateral shift (ft), S = posted speed (mph). Common devices: Type III barricades, channelizing drums, cones, arrow boards, temporary signs. Flaggers: must be trained and certified. Flagger uniform: Class 3 ANSI vest, hard hat, sign paddle (STOP and SLOW) or flag. Pilot car: used on two-lane roads with one-way alternating traffic through work zone. Traffic control plan (TCP): required by most agencies, signed by PE on arterial roads. Detour: when road must be fully closed, provide approved detour route and sign it completely from beginning to end.

chunks.title: Manhole Installation -- Precast Concrete Specs and Construction
chunks.content: Precast concrete manholes per ASTM C478. Standard diameter: 48-inch for sewers up to 24-inch pipe, 60-inch for larger. Sections: base section (with integral bench and channels), riser sections (24-inch height typical), cone section or flat top, grade rings to final grade, frame and cover. Barrel joints: plastic or rubber gasket per ASTM C443. Infiltration test: 2,000 gallons per inch-diameter per mile per day allowable, or air test per ASTM C1244. Pipe connections: cored or cast-in-place, with flexible boot (ASTM C923) -- never rigid concrete at pipe-manhole joint. Benchwork: half-channels matching pipe diameter, smooth concrete bench sloped to channel. Setting: base on 6-inch granular bedding, plumb barrel, adjust grade rings to match final grade. Frame and cover: set in mortar or HDPE adjustment rings, traffic-rated (H-20) in roadways. Grout or plug all lift holes before backfilling.

chunks.title: Equipment Ownership and Operating Cost -- Owning vs Renting
chunks.content: Equipment ownership cost has two components: ownership (fixed) and operating (variable). Ownership cost: depreciation, interest, insurance, storage. Operating cost: fuel, lubrication, tires, maintenance and repair. The AED (Associated Equipment Distributors) Green Book publishes annual ownership and operating cost tables by equipment type. Rule of thumb: hourly ownership plus operating rate for a Cat 320 excavator is approximately $65-$90/hr at full utilization. Rental is often economic at utilization below 60% annually. Break-even example: if you rent at $1,200/day but own at $400/day all-in amortized, you need 60% or greater utilization to justify owning. Idle time kills ownership economics. On bids: use actual owned equipment cost from your records -- do not use rental rate for equipment you own.

chunks.title: Bid Markup Strategy -- Overhead, Profit, and Risk Factors
chunks.content: Bid markup is applied on top of estimated direct costs to cover overhead and return profit. Markup varies by project type, competition, risk, and backlog. Factors to reduce markup: heavy competition, tight market, need for work. Factors to increase markup: sole source or limited bid, specialty work, high risk scope (rock, traffic, deep excavation), tight schedule, difficult owner or engineer. Overhead rate: calculate actual company overhead (office, management, insurance, vehicles, equipment ownership, bonding) as a percentage of direct field cost. If overhead runs 20% and you want 10% profit, total markup on direct cost is 33%. Common mistake: markup as percentage of bid price versus percentage of cost -- these are different. $100 cost x 1.33 = $133 bid. $33 markup is 33% of cost but only 25% of bid price. Bond cost: typically 1.0-2.5% depending on project size and contractor capacity.
